title: Psalms 50
subtitle: The Mighty One, God, the LORD, Speaks

sections.header: Verses 1–15 — “I Have No Need of a Bull from Your Stall”

heb.paragraph: "The Mighty One (ʾēl), God, the LORD, speaks and summons the earth from the rising of the sun to where it sets" (v.1). An Asaph psalm. God arrives in fire and tempest (vv.2–3), calls heaven and earth as witnesses (v.4), and addresses his covenant people (v.5). His message: "I have no need of a bull from your stall" (v.9). God owns every animal (vv.10–11). "If I were hungry I would not tell you" (v.12). What God wants is not sacrifice but thanksgiving and fulfilled vows (vv.14–15).

notes.note: "The Mighty One, God, the LORD speaks." Three divine names stacked: El, Elohim, YHWH. The full weight of divinity speaks.
"I have no need of a bull from your stall. Every animal of the forest is mine. If I were hungry I would not tell you." God is self-sufficient. Sacrifice was never about feeding God.

"Sacrifice thank offerings to God; fulfil your vows to the Most High. Call on me in the day of trouble; I will deliver you, and you will honour me." What God wants: gratitude, fidelity, and dependence.

sections.header: Verses 16–23 — “You Thought I Was Exactly Like You”

heb.paragraph: "When you did these things and I kept silent, you thought (dimmîtā) I was exactly like you" (v.21). God rebukes the wicked who recite his laws but reject his discipline (v.17), who consort with thieves and adulterers (vv.18–20). The core error: projecting human silence onto divine indifference. God’s silence is not approval. "Consider this, you who forget God, or I will tear you to pieces" (v.22).

notes.note: "What right have you to recite my laws? You hate my instruction." Religious speech without obedience is hypocrisy. The wicked QUOTE God while ignoring him.
"You thought I was exactly like you." The fundamental theological error: making God in your own image. Divine silence is not divine agreement.
"Those who sacrifice thank offerings honour me, and to the blameless I will show my salvation." The psalm’s conclusion: thanksgiving honours God; blamelessness sees salvation.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: "The Mighty One, God, the LORD speaks…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI bring no charges against you concerning your sacrifices or concerning your burnt offerings, which are ever before me.ESVNot for your sacrifices do I rebuke you; your burnt offerings are continually before me.NIVFrom Zion, perfect in beauty, God shines forth.ESVOut of Zion, the perfection of beauty, God shines forth.

rec.text: ""The Mighty One, God, the LORD speaks." Three divine names stacked: El, Elohim, YHWH. The full weight of divinity speaks."

rows.label: The Rebuke — 50:16–23
rows.text: "You thought I was exactly like you." God’s silence is not approval. Honour me with thanks.

hebtext.note: "The Mighty One (ʾēl), God, the LORD, speaks and summons the earth from the rising of the sun to where it sets" (v.1). An Asaph psalm. God arrives in fire and tempest (vv.2–3), calls heaven and earth as witnesses (v.4), and addresses his covenant people (v.5). His message: "I have no need of a bull from your stall" (v.9). God owns every animal (vv.10–11). "If I were hungry I would not tell you" (v.12). What God wants is not sacrifice but thanksgiving and fulfilled vows (vv.14–15).

"When you did these things and I kept silent, you thought (dimmîtā) I was exactly like you" (v.21). God rebukes the wicked who recite his laws but reject his discipline (v.17), who consort with thieves and adulterers (vv.18–20). The core error: projecting human silence onto divine indifference. God’s silence is not approval. "Consider this, you who forget God, or I will tear you to pieces" (v.22).

themes.note: Judgment peaks: God rebukes his own people. Holiness: God is not like you. Worship: thanksgiving over sacrifice. Sovereignty: God owns everything.
